title: Integration by parts: definite integrals
description: Practice finding definite integrals using the method of integration by parts.

hints:
We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = \ln x~$ and $~dv=x^3\  dx\,$.
Then $~du = \dfrac1xdx~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int x^3dx = \dfrac14x^4\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int^e_1 x^3\ln x\,dx = \ln x\cdot\dfrac14x^4\Bigg]_1^e-\int^e_1\dfrac14x^4\cdot\dfrac1xdx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~~~~~=\dfrac{x^4\ln x}4\Bigg]_1^e-\int^e_1\dfrac{x^3}4dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~~~~~=\dfrac{x^4\ln x}4-\dfrac1{16}x^4\Bigg]^e_1$

$\qquad\qquad\qquad~~~~~~=\Bigg(\dfrac {e^4}4-\dfrac{e^4}{16}\Bigg)-\Bigg(-\dfrac1{16}\Bigg) = \dfrac{3e^4}{16}+\dfrac1{16}\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=\sin(2x)\, dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int \sin(2x)\,dx = -\dfrac{\cos(2x)}{2}\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int^\pi_0x\sin(2x)\,dx = x\cdot\Big(-\dfrac{\cos(2x)}{2}\Big)\Bigg]_0^\pi-\int^\pi_0-\dfrac{\cos(2x)}{2}dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~~=  -\dfrac{x\cos(2x)}{2}+\bigg(\dfrac{\sin(2x)}4\bigg)\Bigg]^\pi_0$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~~=\Bigg(-\dfrac{\pi\,\cos(2\pi)}{2}+\dfrac{\sin(2\pi)}{4}\Bigg)$

$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad-\Bigg(-\dfrac{\pi \cdot\,\cos\,0}{2}+\dfrac{\sin\,0}{4}\Bigg)$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~~=-\dfrac{\pi}{2}+0 = -\dfrac{\pi}2$

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=e^{4x} dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int e^{4x}dx = \dfrac14e^{4x}\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int^2_0 xe^{4x}dx = x\cdot\dfrac14e^{4x}\Bigg]_0^2-\int^2_0\dfrac14e^{4x}dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~\,=\dfrac x4e^{4x}-\dfrac1{16}e^{4x}\Bigg]^2_0 $

$\qquad\qquad\qquad~\,= \bigg(\dfrac24e^8-\dfrac1{16}e^8\bigg)-\bigg(0-\dfrac1{16}\bigg)$

$\qquad\qquad\qquad~\,\displaystyle=\dfrac7{16}e^8+\dfrac1{16}\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v~^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u~^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=\cos x\, dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \sin x\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad-\int^\pi_{\pi/2} x\cos x\,dx = -\bigg(x\sin x-\int\sin x\,dx\bigg)\Bigg|^\pi_{\pi/2}$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~~=-\big(x\sin x+\cos x\big)\bigg]^\pi_{\pi/2}$

$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~~ = -x\sin x-\cos x\bigg]^\pi_{\pi/2}$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~~=\Big(-\pi\sin\pi-\cos\pi\Big)-\Big(-\dfrac\pi2\sin\dfrac\pi2-\cos\dfrac\pi2\Big)$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~~=\big(0-(-1)\big)-\Big(-\dfrac\pi2-0\Big)= 1+\dfrac\pi2\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=e^{-x} dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int e^{-x}dx = -e^{-x}$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int^5_0 xe^{-x}dx =x\cdot\big(-e^{-x}\big)\Bigg]_0^5-\int^5_0\big(-e^{-x}\big)dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~~= \Big(-xe^{-x}-e^{-x}\Big)\Bigg]^5_0 $

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~~= -e^{-x}(x+1)\Bigg]^5_0$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~~=-6e^{-5}-(-1\cdot1)=-6e^{-5}+1$

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = \ln x~$ and $~dv= \dfrac1{x^2}\,dx\,$.
Then $~du = \dfrac1x\,dx~$ and $~v = -\dfrac1x\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad-2\int^e_1 \dfrac{\ln x}{x^2}\,dx =-2\Big(-\dfrac{\ln x}x\Bigg]_1^e+\int^e_1\dfrac1{x^2}dx\Big)$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad\,=\dfrac{2\ln x}x+\dfrac2x\Bigg]^e_1 $

$\qquad\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\,=\Big(\dfrac2e+\dfrac2e\Big)-\Big(0+2\Big) = \dfrac4e-2$

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=\sqrt{x-t}\ dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \dfrac23(x-t)^{3/2}$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int^t_{0} x\sqrt{x-t}\,dx = x\cdot\dfrac23(x-t)^{3/2}\Bigg|_0^t-\int^t_{0}\dfrac23(x-t)^{3/2}dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~=x\cdot\dfrac23(x-t)^{3/2}-\dfrac23\cdot\dfrac25(x-t)^{5/2}\Bigg]^t_{0}$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~=x\cdot\dfrac23(x-t)^{3/2}-\dfrac4{15}(x-t)^{5/2}\Bigg]^t_{0}$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~=0-\Big(0-\dfrac4{15}(-t)^{5/2}\Big)=\dfrac{4}{15}(-t)^{5/2}$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=\cos(\pi x) dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $\displaystyle~ v =\int\cos(\pi x)\,dx=  \dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}\pi\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int^1_{1/2} x\cos(\pi x)\ dx = \dfrac{x\sin(\pi x)}{\pi}\Bigg]^1_{1/2}-\int^1_{1/2}\dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi}\,dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~=  \dfrac{x\sin(\pi x)}{\pi}-\frac1\pi \Big(-\dfrac{\cos(\pi x)}{\pi}\Big)\Bigg]^1_{1/2}$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~=  \dfrac{x\sin(\pi x)}{\pi}+\dfrac{\cos(\pi x)}{\pi^2}\Bigg]^1_{1/2} $

$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~= \Big(0-\dfrac{1}{\pi^2}\Big)-\Big(\dfrac1{2\pi}+0\Big)=- \dfrac1{2\pi}-\dfrac1{\pi^2}\,$.

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = \ln x~$ and $~dv= \dfrac1{x^3}\,dx\,$.
Then $~du = \dfrac1xdx~$ and $~v = -\dfrac1{2x^2}\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int^e_1 \dfrac{\ln x}{x^3}dx =-\dfrac{\ln x}{2x^2}\Bigg]_1^e+\int^e_1\dfrac1{2x^3}dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~=-\dfrac{\ln x}{2x^2}-\dfrac1{4x^2}\Bigg]^e_1=\Bigg(-\dfrac{1}{2e^2}-\dfrac1{4e^2}\Bigg)-\Bigg(0-\dfrac{1}{4}\Bigg)$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad~~=\dfrac{-3+e^2}{4e^2}$

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=\cos x \,dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \sin x\,$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int^\pi_{3\pi/2} x\cos x\,dx = x\sin x-\int^\pi_{3\pi/2}\sin x\,dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~=x\sin x+\cos x\Bigg]^\pi_{3\pi/2}$

$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~\displaystyle=\pi\sin\pi+\cos\pi-\Big(\frac{3\pi}2\sin\frac{3\pi}2+\cos\frac{3\pi}2\Big)$

$\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~\displaystyle=0-1-\Big(-\dfrac{3\pi}{2}+0\Big) =\dfrac{3\pi}2-1$

We will solve this by integrating by parts. We know that 

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u(x)v\,^\prime(x)dx = u(x)v(x)-\int u\,^\prime(x)v(x)dx\,$.

We can rewrite this as

$\qquad\displaystyle\int u\ dv = uv-\int v\ du\,$.
In this problem we will let $~u = x~$ and $~dv=\sin\Big(\dfrac x2\Big)\, dx\,$.
Then $~du = dx~$ and $~v = \displaystyle\int \sin\Big(\dfrac x2\Big)\,dx = -2\,{\cos\Big(\dfrac x2\Big)}$.

Integration by parts gives

$\displaystyle\qquad\int^{2\pi}_\pi x\sin\Big(\dfrac x2\Big) dx= x\cdot\Bigg(-2\cos\Big(\dfrac x2\Big)\Bigg)\Bigg]_\pi^{2\pi}-\int^{2\pi}_\pi-2\cos\Big(\dfrac x2\Big)dx$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~~~~~= -2x\,\cos\Big(\dfrac x2\Big)+4\sin\Big(\dfrac x2\Big)\Bigg]^{2\pi}_\pi$

$\displaystyle\qquad\qquad\qquad\qquad~~~~~=\Big(4\pi+0\Big)-\Big(0+4\Big) = 4\pi-4\,$.
